content:
# James Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 263 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/JAS_002_page_001.jpg`

---

¹ My brothers and sisters, how can you claim to have faith in our glorious Lord Jesus Christ and still play favorites with people?

² Imagine this: a man walks into your gathering wearing gold rings and expensive clothes, and right behind him comes a poor man dressed in filthy rags.

³ If you lavish attention on the well-dressed man and say, "Here, take this excellent seat," but tell the poor man, "Stand over there," or, "Sit on the floor by my feet"—

⁴ haven't you created divisions among yourselves and become judges with corrupt motives?

⁵ Listen carefully, my dear brothers and sisters: didn't God choose the people the world considers poor to be rich in faith and to inherit the kingdom he promised to those who love him?

⁶ But you have humiliated the poor! And who is it that exploits you? Who drags you into court? Isn't it the wealthy?

⁷ Aren't they the ones who slander the noble name by which you have been called?

⁸ If you truly keep the royal law found in Scripture—"Love your neighbor as yourself"—you are doing well.

⁹ But if you show favoritism, you are committing sin, and the law exposes you as lawbreakers.

¹⁰ For whoever keeps the whole law but stumbles at just one point has become guilty of breaking all of it.

¹¹ The same God who said, "Do not commit adultery," also said, "Do not murder." So even if you have never committed adultery but you murder someone, you have still become a lawbreaker.

¹² Speak and act as people who are going to be judged by the law that sets people free.

¹³ For judgment will be merciless toward anyone who has shown no mercy. But mercy always triumphs over judgment!

¹⁴ What good is it, my brothers and sisters, if someone claims to have faith but has nothing to show for it? Can that kind of faith save anyone?

¹⁵ Suppose a brother or sister has no clothes to wear and nothing to eat today.

¹⁶ If one of you tells them, "Go in peace! Stay warm and eat well," but does nothing to meet their physical needs—what good is that?

¹⁷ In the same way, faith that stands alone—without any action to back it up—is dead.

¹⁸ But someone will say, "You have faith and I have actions." Show me your faith without any actions, and I will show you my faith by what I do.

¹⁹ You believe that God is one? Good for you! Even the demons believe that—and they shudder in terror.

²⁰ Do you want proof, you foolish person, that faith without actions is useless?

²¹ Wasn't our ancestor Abraham shown to be right with God by what he did when he offered his son Isaac on the altar?

²² Can you see it? His faith and his actions were working hand in hand, and his faith was made complete by what he did.

²³ And so the Scripture was fulfilled that says, "Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness"—and he was called God's friend.

²⁴ You see, then, that a person is made right with God by what they do, and not by faith alone.

²⁵ In the same way, wasn't Rahab the prostitute also shown to be right with God by what she did—when she welcomed the spies and helped them escape by a different route?

²⁶ Just as a body without breath is dead, so faith without action is dead.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **"Play favorites" (v. 1):** The Greek προσωπολημψία (prosopolempsia) literally means "receiving the face"—judging by outward appearance. I used "play favorites" because it captures the social dynamic James is attacking: not merely noticing differences, but actively treating people unequally because of them.

2. **"Gold rings and expensive clothes" (v. 2):** The Greek χρυσοδακτύλιος (chrysodaktylios) means "gold-fingered"—wearing gold rings was a conspicuous status marker in the Roman world. James paints a vivid courtroom of social bias right inside the assembly.

3. **"Judges with corrupt motives" (v. 4):** The Greek κριταὶ διαλογισμῶν πονηρῶν means literally "judges of evil reasonings." I rendered this as "judges with corrupt motives" to make clear that the problem is not merely poor judgment but morally compromised thinking.

4. **"The royal law" (v. 8):** James calls the love command νόμον βασιλικόν—the "kingly" or "royal" law. This is not just one rule among many; it is the supreme law, the law of the Kingdom itself, from which all other commandments flow.

5. **"The law that sets people free" (v. 12):** The Greek νόμου ἐλευθερίας ("law of freedom/liberty") is a striking paradox. Law normally constrains; this law liberates. I chose "the law that sets people free" to make the paradox land in English as forcefully as it does in Greek.

6. **"Mercy always triumphs over judgment" (v. 13):** The Greek κατακαυχᾶται (katakauchaomai) means "boasts against" or "exults over." Mercy does not merely soften judgment—it overpowers it, stands over it in victory. I used "triumphs" to convey that dominance.

7. **"Faith without action is dead" (vv. 17, 20, 26):** James hammers this thesis three times with increasing force. The Greek νεκρά/ἀργή means "dead" and "idle/useless." The repetition is not redundant—it is rhetorical escalation. I preserved the refrain to maintain James's drumbeat structure.

8. **"They shudder in terror" (v. 19):** The Greek φρίσσουσιν (phrissousin) means to bristle with fear, to have one's hair stand on end. The demons' belief produces not obedience or love but raw, visceral horror. This is James's devastating point: mere intellectual belief, without transformed action, puts you in the company of demons.

9. **"Faith and actions working hand in hand" (v. 22):** The Greek συνήργει (synergei)—from which we get "synergy"—means "worked together with." James is not pitting faith against works; he is arguing they are inseparable partners. Abraham's faith was not replaced by works but completed through them.

10. **"A body without breath" (v. 26):** Sinaiticus reads πνεύματος (pneumatos, "spirit/breath") here rather than the later variant "soul." This is James's closing metaphor: just as the animating breath makes a body alive, so action is what makes faith alive. The word πνεῦμα—spirit, breath—is the very word from which PNEUMA takes its name.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
